note_data.title: Superposition
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated as v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object moving in a circular path at constant speed has constantly changing velocity due to direction change, therefore it must be accelerating
Centripetal acceleration always acts perpendicular to motion toward the centre of the circle
Centripetal force can be calculated using F = mv²/r = mω²r
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces
Newton's law of gravitation states gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
Gravitational field strength in a radial field follows g = GM/r², showing an inverse square relationship

definitions.definition: The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
Acceleration experienced by an object moving in a circular path, calculated as a = v²/r = ω²r
Force that always acts perpendicular to motion toward the centre of the circle, causing circular motion
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of points about what happens, often with reference to a diagram
Use given data and relevant equations to prove the stated result

modelAnswer.question: Explain how a stationary wave is formed and describe the positions of nodes and antinodes. [4]
modelAnswer.answer: A stationary wave is formed when two waves of the same frequency and amplitude travel in opposite directions and superpose. At certain points (nodes), the waves always meet in antiphase causing destructive interference, so displacement is always zero. At other points (antinodes), the waves always meet in phase causing constructive interference, so displacement is maximum. The distance between adjacent nodes is λ/2.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Two waves travel in opposite directions and superpose
Mark 2: Nodes form where waves meet in antiphase/destructive interference occurs
Mark 3: Antinodes form where waves meet in phase/constructive interference occurs
Mark 4: Distance between adjacent nodes = λ/2

commonMistakes: Forgetting that objects in uniform circular motion are accelerating despite constant speed
Confusing linear speed with angular speed when applying circular motion formulas
Not recognizing that centripetal force always acts toward the center, perpendicular to velocity
Misapplying the inverse square law - if distance doubles, field strength decreases by factor of 4, not 2
examinerTips: Remember to convert between degrees and radians by multiplying by π/180 or 180/π respectively
Use the relationship v = ωr to connect linear and angular quantities in circular motion problems
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Stationary wave diagram showing sinusoidal pattern with clearly marked nodes (N) at zero displacement and antinodes (A) at maximum displacement, with λ/2 spacing between adjacent nodes/antinodes
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why two loudspeakers produce regions of constructive and destructive interference, requiring knowledge of path difference and phase relationships
N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing understanding of conditions needed for observable interference patterns with coherent sources
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(b) (4 marks) — students had to calculate positions of maxima and minima for water waves passing through two gaps, using λ and path difference formulas
N 2023 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — comprehensive question on Young's double slit experiment requiring explanation of bright and dark fringes, plus calculation of fringe spacing
M/J 2021 P2 Q6(a) (3 marks) — asked students to state conditions for stationary waves and explain how nodes and antinodes form through superposition

workedExample.question: Two coherent sources S1 and S2 are separated by 4.0 cm and emit water waves of wavelength 2.0 cm. Calculate the path difference at point P which is 8.0 cm from S1 and 6.0 cm from S2. State whether constructive or destructive interference occurs at P. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the distances from each source to point P: d1 = 8.0 cm from S1, d2 = 6.0 cm from S2
Step 2: Calculate path difference = |d1 - d2| = |8.0 - 6.0| = 2.0 cm
Step 3: Compare path difference to wavelength: path difference = 2.0 cm, wavelength λ = 2.0 cm
Step 4: Since path difference = nλ where n = 1 (whole number), constructive interference occurs at point P
workedExample.answer: Path difference = 2.0 cm. Since this equals one wavelength (nλ where n=1), constructive interference occurs at point P, producing maximum amplitude.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct path difference calculation, 1 mark for comparing to wavelength, 1 mark for identifying constructive interference, 1 mark for correct reasoning about nλ condition

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused path difference with phase difference' — always state path difference in terms of wavelengths (nλ or (n+½)λ) when determining interference type

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot that sources must be coherent for observable interference' — always mention coherent sources (same frequency and constant phase relationship) in interference explanations

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses failed to distinguish between superposition and interference' — remember superposition is the general principle, interference is the specific result when waves overlap
note_data.keyDiagram: Essential diagram shows two wave sources S1 and S2 producing circular wavefronts. Draw solid lines for wave crests, dashed lines for troughs. Mark point P with two path lines to each source, labeling distances d1 and d2. Include regions of constructive interference (where crests meet crests) and destructive interference (where crests meet troughs). Add arrows showing wave direction and label wavelength λ on the wavefronts.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement measured in radians and angular speed related to linear speed by v = ωr. Objects in circular motion experience centripetal acceleration toward the center, requiring centripetal force calculated as F = mv²/r. Gravitational fields create forces between masses following Newton's inverse square law, with field strength decreasing as 1/r².
